text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 27 June 2018 

Dr Amelia Carter  
St Bartholomew’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE  

Dear Dr Amelia Carter,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-02-1990, Hospital No: N374820 NHS No. 9034578216  
Flat 2B, 18 Aldersgate Street, London EC1A 4HJ 

Diagnoses: 

Generalised epilepsy with history of generalised tonic-clonic and nocturnal seizures; Vagus Nerve Stimulator (VNS) in situ. 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g bd  
Clobazam 10 mg nocte  
Vagus Nerve Stimulator active (no changes made today)

Plan: 

- Continue current antiepileptic regimen without changes  
- Maintain VNS parameters at current settings; no adjustments required today  
- Reinforce seizure diary use and adherence to regular sleep routine  
- Safety counselling updated (no unsupervised bathing, caution with heights/open water)  
- Review in 6 months, or sooner if seizure pattern changes or adverse effects occur

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today accompanied by his key worker. He has a confirmed diagnosis of generalised epilepsy, supported by prior EEG evidence of generalised spike–wave activity and a longstanding clinical history. He has an implanted VNS, which was interrogated today; diagnostics were normal and there were no parameter changes at this review.

Regarding seizures this year, his carer and seizure diary together document the following: In Jan a generalised tonic-clonic seizure were reported. In Mar seven nocturnal seizures occurred, and in Jun another during respite care. No emergency treatment was required, and recovery was uncomplicated on each occasion. There have been no reported daytime absences or myoclonic jerks. Identified triggers include sleep disruption around shift changes; alcohol is avoided.

Medication adherence is reported as good with levetiracetam 1 g twice daily and clobazam 10 mg at night. He denies dose omissions or adverse effects beyond mild daytime fatigue, which he finds manageable. Given the current pattern and acceptable tolerability, I have not altered his medications. We discussed that the VNS is functioning as expected, and since the recent cluster was temporally associated with disrupted sleep, we agreed to focus on sleep regularity before considering any parameter changes.

He and his support team will continue the seizure diary, particularly noting sleep duration, illness, and stressors. Rescue protocol remains unchanged and is understood by caregivers. Driving is not applicable. Employment support services remain engaged.

I will review him again in six months or earlier if there is an increase in seizure frequency, prolonged recovery, medication side effects, or if caregivers feel VNS adjustments might be beneficial.

With best wishes,

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit counts of seizures for this year: Jan: 1 generalised tonic-clonic; Mar: 7 nocturnal; Jun: 1 during respite care. Total = 1 + 7 + 1 = 9 seizures. The clinic date is 27 June 2018, so these 9 seizures occurred over approximately the first 6 months of the year. According to the rules, use the highest frequency and normalize to allowed formats. Therefore, 9 per 6 months.
